subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Differentiate between 'conduction current' and 'displacement current', giving one similarity and one dissimilarity between them.
  2. Explain the existence of electromagnetic waves in free space, using the concept of displacement current.
question_text: Differentiate between 'conduction current' and 'displacement current', giving one similarity and one dissimilarity between them. Explain the existence of electromagnetic waves in free space, using the concept of displacement current.
explanation: Similarity Both give rise to magnetic field. Dissimilarity Conduction current is due to flow of charges in the conductor. Displacement current arises due to change in electric field/ time varying electric flux. A magnetic field, changing with time, gives rise to an electric field. Then, an electric field changing with time gives rise to a magnetic field and is a consequence of the displacement current being a source of a magnetic field. Thus, time-dependent electric and magnetic fields give rise to each other and hence em wave is generated. {tex}\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}=\mu_0 \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{c}}+\mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\mathrm{d} \Phi_{\mathrm{c}}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex}
solution: Similarity Both give rise to magnetic field. Dissimilarity Conduction current is due to flow of charges in the conductor. Displacement current arises due to change in electric field/ time varying electric flux. A magnetic field, changing with time, gives rise to an electric field. Then, an electric field changing with time gives rise to a magnetic field and is a consequence of the displacement current being a source of a magnetic field. Thus, time-dependent electric and magnetic fields give rise to each other and hence em wave is generated. {tex}\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}=\mu_0 \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{c}}+\mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\mathrm{d} \Phi_{\mathrm{c}}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Similarity
    Both give rise to magnetic field.
    Dissimilarity
    • Conduction current is due to flow of charges in the conductor.
    • Displacement current arises due to change in electric field/ time varying electric flux.
  2. A magnetic field, changing with time, gives rise to an electric field. Then, an electric field changing with time gives rise to a magnetic field and is a consequence of the displacement current being a source of a magnetic field. Thus, time-dependent electric and magnetic fields give rise to each other and hence em wave is generated.
    {tex}\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}=\mu_0 \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{c}}+\mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\mathrm{d} \Phi_{\mathrm{c}}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex}

explanation: Magnetic field oscillates in the east-west direction as it is mutually perpendicular to the direction of electric field. Wavelength of the radiowaves and microwaves is longer than visible light. Use of infrared waves:- Used for physical therapy. Used for maintaining average temperature of the earth. Used in Earth satellites for military purposes & to observe growth of crops. Used in remote control Use of Gamma rays:- Used in treatment of cancer. Used in diagnostic imaging. Used to sterilize medical equipment

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. State any three characteristics of electromagnetic waves.
  2. Briefly explain how and where the displacement current exists during the charging of a capacitor.
question_text: State any three characteristics of electromagnetic waves. Briefly explain how and where the displacement current exists during the charging of a capacitor.
explanation: Electromagnetic wave carries energy. Electromagnetic wave carries momentum. Electromagnetic wave moves with velocity of light in vacuum. In electromagnetic wave, electric field vector, magnetic field vector and direction of propagation, all are mutually perpendicular. Electromagnetic waves are transverse in nature. Electromagnetic waves do not require a physical medium to propagate and can travel through a vacuum. Electromagnetic waves consist of oscillating electric and magnetic fields. During charging of capacitor, time varying electric field/electric flux between the plates of capacitor induces the displacement current. Displacement current exists between the plates of capacitor.
solution: Electromagnetic wave carries energy. Electromagnetic wave carries momentum. Electromagnetic wave moves with velocity of light in vacuum. In electromagnetic wave, electric field vector, magnetic field vector and direction of propagation, all are mutually perpendicular. Electromagnetic waves are transverse in nature. Electromagnetic waves do not require a physical medium to propagate and can travel through a vacuum. Electromagnetic waves consist of oscillating electric and magnetic fields. During charging of capacitor, time varying electric field/electric flux between the plates of capacitor induces the displacement current. Displacement current exists between the plates of capacitor.
solution_html:
• Electromagnetic wave carries energy.
    • Electromagnetic wave carries momentum.
    • Electromagnetic wave moves with velocity of light in vacuum.
    • In electromagnetic wave, electric field vector, magnetic field vector and direction of propagation, all are mutually perpendicular.
    • Electromagnetic waves are transverse in nature.
    • Electromagnetic waves do not require a physical medium to propagate and can travel through a vacuum.
    • Electromagnetic waves consist of oscillating electric and magnetic fields.
    • During charging of capacitor, time varying electric field/electric flux between the plates of capacitor induces the displacement current.
    • Displacement current exists between the plates of capacitor.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. A parallel plate capacitor is charged by an ac source. Show that the sum of conduction current {tex}\left({I}_{{c}}\right){/tex} and the displacement current {tex}\left({I}_{{d}}\right){/tex} has the same value at all points of the circuit.
  2. In case (a) above, is Kirchhoff's first rule(junction rule) valid at each plate of the capacitor? Explain.
question_text: A parallel plate capacitor is charged by an ac source. Show that the sum of conduction current {tex}\left({I}_{{c}}\right){/tex} and the displacement current {tex}\left({I}_{{d}}\right){/tex} has the same value at all points of the circuit. In case (a) above, is Kirchhoff's first rule(junction rule) valid at each plate of the capacitor? Explain.
explanation: {tex}\because{/tex} Total current {tex}{I}={I}_{{c}}+{I}_{{d}}{/tex} outside the capacitor {tex}{I}_{{d}} =0{/tex} {tex}\therefore {I} ={I}_{{c}}{/tex} Inside the capacitor {tex}{I}_{{c}}=0{/tex} {tex}\therefore {I}={I}_{{d}} =\varepsilon_{0} \frac{d \phi_{E}}{d t}{/tex} {tex}=\varepsilon_{0} \frac{d}{d t}[E A]{/tex} {tex}=\varepsilon_{0} \frac{d}{d t}\left[\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}} A\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}}{\varepsilon_{0}} A \frac{d}{d t}\left[\frac{Q}{A}\right]{/tex} {tex}{I} =\frac{d Q}{d t}={I}_{{c}}{/tex} Yes Current entering the capacitor is {tex}\left({I}_{{c}}\right){/tex} and between the plates capacitor is {tex}\left({I}_{{d}}\right){/tex} {tex}I_{c}=I_{d}{/tex} which validates Kirchhoff's junction rule.
solution: {tex}\because{/tex} Total current {tex}{I}={I}_{{c}}+{I}_{{d}}{/tex} outside the capacitor {tex}{I}_{{d}} =0{/tex} {tex}\therefore {I} ={I}_{{c}}{/tex} Inside the capacitor {tex}{I}_{{c}}=0{/tex} {tex}\therefore {I}={I}_{{d}} =\varepsilon_{0} \frac{d \phi_{E}}{d t}{/tex} {tex}=\varepsilon_{0} \frac{d}{d t}[E A]{/tex} {tex}=\varepsilon_{0} \frac{d}{d t}\left[\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}} A\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}}{\varepsilon_{0}} A \frac{d}{d t}\left[\frac{Q}{A}\right]{/tex} {tex}{I} =\frac{d Q}{d t}={I}_{{c}}{/tex} Yes Current entering the capacitor is {tex}\left({I}_{{c}}\right){/tex} and between the plates capacitor is {tex}\left({I}_{{d}}\right){/tex} {tex}I_{c}=I_{d}{/tex} which validates Kirchhoff's junction rule.
solution_html:
1.  {tex}\because{/tex} Total current {tex}{I}={I}_{{c}}+{I}_{{d}}{/tex}
    outside the capacitor
    {tex}{I}_{{d}} =0{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore {I} ={I}_{{c}}{/tex}
    Inside the capacitor
    {tex}{I}_{{c}}=0{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore {I}={I}_{{d}} =\varepsilon_{0} \frac{d \phi_{E}}{d t}{/tex}
    {tex}=\varepsilon_{0} \frac{d}{d t}[E A]{/tex}
    {tex}=\varepsilon_{0} \frac{d}{d t}\left[\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}} A\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}}{\varepsilon_{0}} A \frac{d}{d t}\left[\frac{Q}{A}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}{I} =\frac{d Q}{d t}={I}_{{c}}{/tex}
  2. Yes
    Current entering the capacitor is {tex}\left({I}_{{c}}\right){/tex} and between the plates capacitor is {tex}\left({I}_{{d}}\right){/tex}
    {tex}I_{c}=I_{d}{/tex}
    which validates Kirchhoff's junction rule.

subject: Physics
question_html: A laser beam of wavelength 500 nm and power 5 mW strikes normally on a perfectly reflecting surface of area {tex}1 {mm}^{2}{/tex} of a body. It rebounds back from the surface. Find the force exerted by the laser beam on the body.
question_text: A laser beam of wavelength 500 nm and power 5 mW strikes normally on a perfectly reflecting surface of area {tex}1 {mm}^{2}{/tex} of a body. It rebounds back from the surface. Find the force exerted by the laser beam on the body.
explanation: Finding force exerted by laser beam No. of photons ejected per second {tex}=\frac{P}{E}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{P}{\left(\frac{h c}{\lambda}\right)}{/tex} Momentum of photon, {tex}p=\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex} Force exerted by laser beam {tex}F =2 \times \frac{P}{\left(\frac{h c}{\lambda}\right)} \times \frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 P}{c}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}{3 \times 10^{8}}{/tex} {tex}F =3.33 \times 10^{-11} {~N}{/tex}
solution: Finding force exerted by laser beam No. of photons ejected per second {tex}=\frac{P}{E}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{P}{\left(\frac{h c}{\lambda}\right)}{/tex} Momentum of photon, {tex}p=\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex} Force exerted by laser beam {tex}F =2 \times \frac{P}{\left(\frac{h c}{\lambda}\right)} \times \frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 P}{c}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}{3 \times 10^{8}}{/tex} {tex}F =3.33 \times 10^{-11} {~N}{/tex}
solution_html:
Finding force exerted by laser beam
No. of photons ejected per second {tex}=\frac{P}{E}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{P}{\left(\frac{h c}{\lambda}\right)}{/tex}
Momentum of photon, {tex}p=\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex}
Force exerted by laser beam
{tex}F =2 \times \frac{P}{\left(\frac{h c}{\lambda}\right)} \times \frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2 P}{c}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}{3 \times 10^{8}}{/tex}
{tex}F =3.33 \times 10^{-11} {~N}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. The wavelength of the electromagnetic wave is often correlated with the characteristic size of the system that radiates. Give two examples to justify this statement.
    1. Long distance radio broadcasts use short-wave bands. Why?
    2. Optical and radio telescopes are built on the ground, but X-ray astronomy is possible only from satellites orbiting the Earth. Why?
question_text: The wavelength of the electromagnetic wave is often correlated with the characteristic size of the system that radiates. Give two examples to justify this statement. Long distance radio broadcasts use short-wave bands. Why? Optical and radio telescopes are built on the ground, but X-ray astronomy is possible only from satellites orbiting the Earth. Why?
explanation: Radio Antennas: Long radio antennas are sized to match the wavelength of the broadcast signals they handle, enhancing signal efficiency and reception. Optical Telescopes: Large mirrors in optical telescopes match the wavelength of visible light to improve resolution and light-gathering capability. Short-Wave Bands: Short-wave radio signals (3 to 30 MHz) are used for long-distance broadcasts because they can reflect off the ionosphere, a layer of the Earth's atmosphere. This reflection allows them to travel beyond the horizon and cover vast distances, making them suitable for international broadcasts. Long-wave signals, on the other hand, tend to follow the curvature of the Earth and do not reflect as effectively, making them less suitable for long-distance communication. Optical and Radio Telescopes: These telescopes are built on the ground because visible light and radio waves can pass through the Earth's atmosphere. Optical telescopes collect visible light, while radio telescopes detect radio waves. The atmosphere does not significantly obstruct these wavelengths, so ground-based observatories can be very effective. X-Ray Astronomy: X-rays do not penetrate the Earth's atmosphere; they are absorbed by it. Therefore, X-ray telescopes must be placed in space to avoid atmospheric absorption. Satellites orbiting Earth provide the necessary environment to observe cosmic X-rays directly. This is why X-ray astronomy is conducted from space-based observatories rather than ground-based facilities.
solution: Radio Antennas: Long radio antennas are sized to match the wavelength of the broadcast signals they handle, enhancing signal efficiency and reception. Optical Telescopes: Large mirrors in optical telescopes match the wavelength of visible light to improve resolution and light-gathering capability. Short-Wave Bands: Short-wave radio signals (3 to 30 MHz) are used for long-distance broadcasts because they can reflect off the ionosphere, a layer of the Earth's atmosphere. This reflection allows them to travel beyond the horizon and cover vast distances, making them suitable for international broadcasts. Long-wave signals, on the other hand, tend to follow the curvature of the Earth and do not reflect as effectively, making them less suitable for long-distance communication. Optical and Radio Telescopes: These telescopes are built on the ground because visible light and radio waves can pass through the Earth's atmosphere. Optical telescopes collect visible light, while radio telescopes detect radio waves. The atmosphere does not significantly obstruct these wavelengths, so ground-based observatories can be very effective. X-Ray Astronomy: X-rays do not penetrate the Earth's atmosphere; they are absorbed by it. Therefore, X-ray telescopes must be placed in space to avoid atmospheric absorption. Satellites orbiting Earth provide the necessary environment to observe cosmic X-rays directly. This is why X-ray astronomy is conducted from space-based observatories rather than ground-based facilities.

1. Radio Antennas: Long radio antennas are sized to match the wavelength of the broadcast signals they handle, enhancing signal efficiency and reception.
    Optical Telescopes: Large mirrors in optical telescopes match the wavelength of visible light to improve resolution and light-gathering capability.
    1. Short-Wave Bands: Short-wave radio signals (3 to 30 MHz) are used for long-distance broadcasts because they can reflect off the ionosphere, a layer of the Earth's atmosphere. This reflection allows them to travel beyond the horizon and cover vast distances, making them suitable for international broadcasts. Long-wave signals, on the other hand, tend to follow the curvature of the Earth and do not reflect as effectively, making them less suitable for long-distance communication.
    2. Optical and Radio Telescopes: These telescopes are built on the ground because visible light and radio waves can pass through the Earth's atmosphere. Optical telescopes collect visible light, while radio telescopes detect radio waves. The atmosphere does not significantly obstruct these wavelengths, so ground-based observatories can be very effective.
      X-Ray Astronomy: X-rays do not penetrate the Earth's atmosphere; they are absorbed by it. Therefore, X-ray telescopes must be placed in space to avoid atmospheric absorption. Satellites orbiting Earth provide the necessary environment to observe cosmic X-rays directly. This is why X-ray astronomy is conducted from space-based observatories rather than ground-based facilities.

question_html:
1. On what factors does the speed of an electromagnetic wave in a medium depend?
  2. How is an electromagnetic wave produced?
  3. Sketch a schematic diagram depicting the electric and magnetic fields for an electromagnetic wave propagating along z-axis.
question_text: On what factors does the speed of an electromagnetic wave in a medium depend? How is an electromagnetic wave produced? Sketch a schematic diagram depicting the electric and magnetic fields for an electromagnetic wave propagating along z-axis.
explanation: The speed of an electromagnetic wave in a medium depends on the medium's permittivity ( {tex}\epsilon{/tex} ) and permeability {tex}(\mu){/tex} . The relationship is given by {tex}v=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\epsilon \mu}}{/tex} . Higher permittivity or permeability typically reduces the speed of the wave. An electromagnetic wave is produced when charged particles, such as electrons, accelerate. This acceleration generates changing electric fields, which in turn create changing magnetic fields. These fields propagate through space as waves, allowing the transmission of energy and information without the need for a physical medium.
solution: The speed of an electromagnetic wave in a medium depends on the medium's permittivity ( {tex}\epsilon{/tex} ) and permeability {tex}(\mu){/tex} . The relationship is given by {tex}v=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\epsilon \mu}}{/tex} . Higher permittivity or permeability typically reduces the speed of the wave. An electromagnetic wave is produced when charged particles, such as electrons, accelerate. This acceleration generates changing electric fields, which in turn create changing magnetic fields. These fields propagate through space as waves, allowing the transmission of energy and information without the need for a physical medium.

1. The speed of an electromagnetic wave in a medium depends on the medium's permittivity ( {tex}\epsilon{/tex} ) and permeability {tex}(\mu){/tex}. The relationship is given by {tex}v=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\epsilon \mu}}{/tex}. Higher permittivity or permeability typically reduces the speed of the wave.
  2. An electromagnetic wave is produced when charged particles, such as electrons, accelerate. This acceleration generates changing electric fields, which in turn create changing magnetic fields. These fields propagate through space as waves, allowing the transmission of energy and information without the need for a physical medium.

What is the amplitude of the magnetic field of the wave? Write an expression for the magnetic field of this wave.

solution: {tex} k =\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} {/tex} {tex} \lambda =\frac{2 \pi}{K}=\frac{4 \pi}{3} {~m}=4.18 {~m} {/tex} {tex} \omega =2 \pi v {/tex} {tex} v =\frac{\omega}{2 \pi}=\frac{4.5 \times 10^8}{2 \pi} {~Hz} {/tex} {tex} v =\frac{9}{4 \pi} \times 10^8 {~Hz} {/tex} {tex} v =7.16 \times 10^{-1} {~Hz} {/tex} {tex} B_0 =\frac{E_0}{c} {/tex} {tex} B_0 =\frac{6.3}{3 \times 10^8}=2.1 \times 10^{-8} {~T} {/tex} {tex} \vec{B} =2.1 \times 10^{-8}{/tex} {tex}\left[(\cos 1.5 {rad} / {m}) {y}+\left(4.5 \times 10^8 {rad} / {s}\right) {t}\right] {k} {T} {/tex}

    {tex} \omega =2 \pi v {/tex}

question_html: What is meant by displacement current? How is this current different from the conduction current? A capacitor is being charged by a source of emf. Justify the continuity of current in the circuit.
question_text: What is meant by displacement current? How is this current different from the conduction current? A capacitor is being charged by a source of emf. Justify the continuity of current in the circuit.
explanation: Displacement current is the current which arises due to rate of change of electric field. Displacement current is due to varying electric field. Conduction current is due to motion of electrons in the presence of electric field. When the capacitor is being charged by a source of emf , the electric field between the plates of capacitor changes with time. It produces a displacement current {tex}{i}_{{d}}{/tex} whose magnitude is equal to conduction current {tex}{i}_{{c}}{/tex} . Therefore the current is continious in the circuit.
solution: Displacement current is the current which arises due to rate of change of electric field. Displacement current is due to varying electric field. Conduction current is due to motion of electrons in the presence of electric field. When the capacitor is being charged by a source of emf , the electric field between the plates of capacitor changes with time. It produces a displacement current {tex}{i}_{{d}}{/tex} whose magnitude is equal to conduction current {tex}{i}_{{c}}{/tex} . Therefore the current is continious in the circuit.
solution_html:
Displacement current is the current which arises due to rate of change of electric field.
Displacement current is due to varying electric field.
Conduction current is due to motion of electrons in the presence of electric field.
When the capacitor is being charged by a source of emf , the electric field between the plates of capacitor changes with time. It produces a displacement current {tex}{i}_{{d}}{/tex} whose magnitude is equal to conduction current {tex}{i}_{{c}}{/tex}. Therefore the current is continious in the circuit.

question_html:
Explain the following, giving proper reason:

  1. During charging of a capacitor, displacement current exists in the capacitor. But there is no displacement current when it gets fully charged.
  2. The frequency of microwaves in ovens matches with the resonant frequency of water molecules.
  3. Infrared waves are also known as heat waves.
question_text: Explain the following, giving proper reason: During charging of a capacitor, displacement current exists in the capacitor. But there is no displacement current when it gets fully charged. The frequency of microwaves in ovens matches with the resonant frequency of water molecules. Infrared waves are also known as heat waves.
explanation: {tex}{i}_{{d}}=\varepsilon_{0} \frac{{~d} \varphi_{{E}}}{{dt}}{/tex} Displacement current is due to changing electric flux. During charging of capacitor, there is change in electric flux. When fully charged electric field hence electric flux does not change. Hence no displacement current. The frequency of the microwaves is selected to match the resonant frequency of water molecules so that energy from the waves is transferred efficiently to the kinetic energy of the molecules. Infrared waves are also known as heat waves, because water molecules present in most materials readily absorbs infrared waves and their thermal motion increases and heat up.
solution: {tex}{i}_{{d}}=\varepsilon_{0} \frac{{~d} \varphi_{{E}}}{{dt}}{/tex} Displacement current is due to changing electric flux. During charging of capacitor, there is change in electric flux. When fully charged electric field hence electric flux does not change. Hence no displacement current. The frequency of the microwaves is selected to match the resonant frequency of water molecules so that energy from the waves is transferred efficiently to the kinetic energy of the molecules. Infrared waves are also known as heat waves, because water molecules present in most materials readily absorbs infrared waves and their thermal motion increases and heat up.
solution_html:
1. {tex}{i}_{{d}}=\varepsilon_{0} \frac{{~d} \varphi_{{E}}}{{dt}}{/tex}
    Displacement current is due to changing electric flux.
    During charging of capacitor, there is change in electric flux.
    When fully charged electric field hence electric flux does not change.
    Hence no displacement current.
  2. The frequency of the microwaves is selected to match the resonant frequency of water molecules so that energy from the waves is transferred efficiently to the kinetic energy of the molecules.
  3. Infrared waves are also known as heat waves, because water molecules present in most materials readily absorbs infrared waves and their thermal motion increases and heat up.

To protect their eyes from large amount of ultraviolet rays produced by welding arcs.

To protect their eyes from large amount of ultraviolet rays produced by welding arcs.
solution_html:
1. Since they have different wavelenghts and frequencies, they differ considerably in their mode of interaction with matter.
  2. Frequency of microwave matches with the resonant frequency of water molecules so that energy from wave is transferred to water molecules.
  3. To protect their eyes from large amount of ultraviolet rays produced by welding arcs.

question_html: What is a displacement current? How is it different from a conduction current?
question_text: What is a displacement current? How is it different from a conduction current?
explanation: Displacement current is the current produced due to changing electric field/electric flux in a region. Difference: Current carried by a conductor due to flow of charges is called conduction current. Displacement current is not due to flow of charges but due to changing electric field/electric flux.
solution: Displacement current is the current produced due to changing electric field/electric flux in a region. Difference: Current carried by a conductor due to flow of charges is called conduction current. Displacement current is not due to flow of charges but due to changing electric field/electric flux.

Displacement current is the current produced due to changing electric field/electric flux in a region.
Difference: Current carried by a conductor due to flow of charges is called conduction current.
Displacement current is not due to flow of charges but due to changing electric field/electric flux.

question_html: How are electromagnetic waves produced? Write their two characteristics.
question_text: How are electromagnetic waves produced? Write their two characteristics.
explanation: An oscillating charge produces an oscillating electric field in space which further produces an oscillating magnetic field which in turn is a source of electric field. These oscillating electric and magnetic field, hence, keep on regenerating each other and an electromagnetic wave is produced Electromagnetic waves have the following characteristics: Electromagnetic waves are propagated by oscillating electric and magnetic fields that are perpendicular to each other. In a vacuum, electromagnetic waves have a constant velocity.
solution: An oscillating charge produces an oscillating electric field in space which further produces an oscillating magnetic field which in turn is a source of electric field. These oscillating electric and magnetic field, hence, keep on regenerating each other and an electromagnetic wave is produced Electromagnetic waves have the following characteristics: Electromagnetic waves are propagated by oscillating electric and magnetic fields that are perpendicular to each other. In a vacuum, electromagnetic waves have a constant velocity.
solution_html:
An oscillating charge produces an oscillating electric field in space which further produces an oscillating magnetic field which in turn is a source of electric field. These oscillating electric and magnetic field, hence, keep on regenerating each other and an electromagnetic wave is produced
Electromagnetic waves have the following characteristics:

  1. Electromagnetic waves are propagated by oscillating electric and magnetic fields that are perpendicular to each other.
  2. In a vacuum, electromagnetic waves have a constant velocity.

question_html:
A plane electromagnetic wave propagates in vacuum along x-axis.

  1. Give the direction of electric field and magnetic field vectors.
  2. E0 and B0 are the magnitudes of electric and magnetic fields respectively in a plane electromagnetic wave. Find the ratio of energy densities of electric field and magnetic field.
question_text: A plane electromagnetic wave propagates in vacuum along x-axis. Give the direction of electric field and magnetic field vectors. E 0 and B 0 are the magnitudes of electric and magnetic fields respectively in a plane electromagnetic wave. Find the ratio of energy densities of electric field and magnetic field.
explanation: Electric field vector and magnetic field vector are along y-axis and z-axis or vice versa. {tex}\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{E}}=\frac{1}{2} \in_o E_o^2 {/tex} {tex}\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B_o^2}{\mu_o}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{u}_E}{\mathrm{u}_B}=\mu_o \in_o \frac{E_o^2}{B_o^2}{/tex} Using {tex}\frac{E_o}{B_o}=c{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{c}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_o \epsilon_o}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{u}_E}{\mathrm{u}_B}=\mu_o \varepsilon_o c^2=1{/tex}
solution: Electric field vector and magnetic field vector are along y-axis and z-axis or vice versa. {tex}\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{E}}=\frac{1}{2} \in_o E_o^2 {/tex} {tex}\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B_o^2}{\mu_o}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{u}_E}{\mathrm{u}_B}=\mu_o \in_o \frac{E_o^2}{B_o^2}{/tex} Using {tex}\frac{E_o}{B_o}=c{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{c}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_o \epsilon_o}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{u}_E}{\mathrm{u}_B}=\mu_o \varepsilon_o c^2=1{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Electric field vector and magnetic field vector are along y-axis and z-axis or vice versa.
  2. {tex}\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{E}}=\frac{1}{2} \in_o E_o^2 {/tex} {tex}\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B_o^2}{\mu_o}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{\mathrm{u}_E}{\mathrm{u}_B}=\mu_o \in_o \frac{E_o^2}{B_o^2}{/tex}
    Using {tex}\frac{E_o}{B_o}=c{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{c}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_o \epsilon_o}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{\mathrm{u}_E}{\mathrm{u}_B}=\mu_o \varepsilon_o c^2=1{/tex}

question_text: Long distance radio broadcasts use short-wave bands. Why? The experimental demonstration of electromagnetic waves is possible only in the low frequency region (the radio wave region). Explain.
explanation: Long-distance radio broadcasts use short wave bands as they are easily reflected by the earth's atmosphere primarily ionosphere and can thus be sent to longer distances. The experimental demonstration of EM waves is possible only with low-frequency regions as the modern electronic circuit oscillates with a frequency around 10 ​​​​​​11, near the frequency of radio waves.

Bz = B0 sin (kx - wt)
Characteristics of EMWs

question_text: Find the ratio of the de Broglie wavelengths associated with an alpha particle and a proton, if both have the same speeds, have the same kinetic energy, are accelerated through the same potential difference.
explanation: {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{p} {/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_\alpha}{\lambda_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{m_\alpha v_\alpha}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{m_p v_p}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} p = {tex}\sqrt{2 m(K . E)}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_\alpha}{\lambda_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_\alpha(\mathrm{K} \cdot \mathrm{E} .)_\alpha}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2 m_p(K . \mathrm{E} \cdot)_p}}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{m_p}{m_\alpha}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} v = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{qV}}{\mathrm{m}}} {/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_\alpha}{\lambda_{\mathrm{p}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{m}_\alpha \mathrm{v}_\alpha}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{p}} \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{h}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{m}_\alpha} \sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{p}} V}{\mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{p}}}} \times \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{m}_\alpha}{2 \mathrm{q}_\alpha V}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{m_p}{m_\alpha}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{\frac{m_\alpha}{m_p}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{\frac{q_p}{q_\alpha}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{p} {/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_\alpha}{\lambda_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{m_\alpha v_\alpha}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{m_p v_p}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} p = {tex}\sqrt{2 m(K . E)}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_\alpha}{\lambda_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_\alpha(\mathrm{K} \cdot \mathrm{E} .)_\alpha}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2 m_p(K . \mathrm{E} \cdot)_p}}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{m_p}{m_\alpha}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} v = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{qV}}{\mathrm{m}}} {/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_\alpha}{\lambda_{\mathrm{p}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{m}_\alpha \mathrm{v}_\alpha}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{p}} \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{h}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{m}_\alpha} \sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{p}} V}{\mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{p}}}} \times \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{m}_\alpha}{2 \mathrm{q}_\alpha V}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{m_p}{m_\alpha}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{\frac{m_\alpha}{m_p}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{\frac{q_p}{q_\alpha}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{p} {/tex}
    {tex}\frac{\lambda_\alpha}{\lambda_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{m_\alpha v_\alpha}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{m_p v_p}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
  2. p = {tex}\sqrt{2 m(K . E)}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{\lambda_\alpha}{\lambda_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_\alpha(\mathrm{K} \cdot \mathrm{E} .)_\alpha}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex}{tex}\frac{\sqrt{2 m_p(K . \mathrm{E} \cdot)_p}}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{m_p}{m_\alpha}}{/tex}= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
  3. v = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{qV}}{\mathrm{m}}} {/tex}
    {tex}\frac{\lambda_\alpha}{\lambda_{\mathrm{p}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{m}_\alpha \mathrm{v}_\alpha}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{p}} \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{h}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{m}_\alpha} \sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{p}} V}{\mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{p}}}} \times \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{m}_\alpha}{2 \mathrm{q}_\alpha V}}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{m_p}{m_\alpha}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex}{tex}\sqrt{\frac{m_\alpha}{m_p}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{\frac{q_p}{q_\alpha}}{/tex}= {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}{/tex}

question_html:
1. Explain briefly the fact that electromagnetic waves carry energy.
  2. Why do we not feel the pressure due to sunshine?
question_text: Explain briefly the fact that electromagnetic waves carry energy. Why do we not feel the pressure due to sunshine?
explanation: When electromagnetic waves fall on charged particles they bring the charges into motion, which generally shows that electromagnetic waves have both energy and momentum. When the sun shines, we feel energy absorbed from electromagnetic waves on our body. Electromagnetic waves also transfer momentum to your hand but because c is very large, the amount of momentum transferred is extremely small and you do not feel the pressure.
solution: When electromagnetic waves fall on charged particles they bring the charges into motion, which generally shows that electromagnetic waves have both energy and momentum. When the sun shines, we feel energy absorbed from electromagnetic waves on our body. Electromagnetic waves also transfer momentum to your hand but because c is very large, the amount of momentum transferred is extremely small and you do not feel the pressure.
solution_html:
1. When electromagnetic waves fall on charged particles they bring the charges into motion, which generally shows that electromagnetic waves have both energy and momentum.
  2. When the sun shines, we feel energy absorbed from electromagnetic waves on our body. Electromagnetic waves also transfer momentum to your hand but because c is very large, the amount of momentum transferred is extremely small and you do not feel the pressure.

question_text: We feel the warmth of the sunlight but not the pressure on our hands. Explain. Which out of wavelength, frequency and speed of an electromagnetic wave does not change on passing from one medium to another? A thin ozone layer in the upper atmosphere is crucial for humans' survival on Earth, why?
explanation: The Sun outputs about 1300 watts per square metre {W/m} 2 in space near the Earth, which gets reduced to around 650 W/m 2 after through the atmosphere. So, the amount of heat is quite realisable. The forces generated by solar radiation is generally too small to be noticed under everyday life. The radiation pressure of sunlight on Earth is equivalent to that exerted by about a thousandth of a gram on an area of 1 square metre. It is approximately 10 {tex}\mu{/tex} Nm 2 . Hence, it is negligible. Wavelength and velocity of electromagnetic wave change from one medium to another medium. So, frequency does not change. Ozone layer absorbs biologically harmful ultraviolet radiation coming from the Sun. Hence, it is crucial for humans' survival on Earth.

question_html:
What are matter waves? Find the ratio of de Broglie wavelengths associated with proton and alpha particles when both particles

  1. are accelerated through the same potential difference.
  2. have same velocity.
question_text: What are matter waves? Find the ratio of de Broglie wavelengths associated with proton and alpha particles when both particles are accelerated through the same potential difference. have same velocity.
explanation: The de Broglie waves associated with moving particles are called matter waves. {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E_k}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_\alpha=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_\alpha q_\alpha V}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_p=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_p q_p V}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_p}{\lambda_\alpha}=\sqrt{\frac{m_\alpha q_\alpha}{m_p q_p}}=\sqrt{\frac{4 m_p 2 q_p}{m_p q_p}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{1}{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{m v}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_p=\frac{h}{m_p v}{/tex} & {tex}\lambda_\alpha=\frac{h}{m_\alpha v}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_p}{\lambda_\alpha}=\frac{m_\alpha}{m_p}=\frac{4}{1}{/tex} hence, the ratio of de Broglie wavelengths associated with proton and alpha particles when both particles have same velocity is 4:1
solution: The de Broglie waves associated with moving particles are called matter waves. {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E_k}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_\alpha=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_\alpha q_\alpha V}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_p=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_p q_p V}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_p}{\lambda_\alpha}=\sqrt{\frac{m_\alpha q_\alpha}{m_p q_p}}=\sqrt{\frac{4 m_p 2 q_p}{m_p q_p}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{1}{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{m v}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_p=\frac{h}{m_p v}{/tex} & {tex}\lambda_\alpha=\frac{h}{m_\alpha v}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_p}{\lambda_\alpha}=\frac{m_\alpha}{m_p}=\frac{4}{1}{/tex} hence, the ratio of de Broglie wavelengths associated with proton and alpha particles when both particles have same velocity is 4:1
solution_html:
The de Broglie waves associated with moving particles are called matter waves.

  1. {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E_k}}{/tex}
    {tex}\lambda_\alpha=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_\alpha q_\alpha V}}{/tex}
    {tex}\lambda_p=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_p q_p V}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{\lambda_p}{\lambda_\alpha}=\sqrt{\frac{m_\alpha q_\alpha}{m_p q_p}}=\sqrt{\frac{4 m_p 2 q_p}{m_p q_p}}{/tex}
  2. = {tex}\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{1}{/tex}
    {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{m v}{/tex}
    {tex}\lambda_p=\frac{h}{m_p v}{/tex} & {tex}\lambda_\alpha=\frac{h}{m_\alpha v}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{\lambda_p}{\lambda_\alpha}=\frac{m_\alpha}{m_p}=\frac{4}{1}{/tex}
    hence, the ratio of de Broglie wavelengths associated with proton and alpha particles when both particles have same velocity is 4:1

question_html:
1. In what ways are matter waves different from electromagnetic waves?
  2. If the kinetic energy of a particle is reduced to one-fourth {tex}\left(\frac{1}{4}\right){/tex} of its initial value, how many times will the de Broglie wavelength associated with it become?
question_text: In what ways are matter waves different from electromagnetic waves? If the kinetic energy of a particle is reduced to one-fourth {tex}\left(\frac{1}{4}\right){/tex} of its initial value, how many times will the de Broglie wavelength associated with it become?
explanation: Matter waves (i) Matter waves are associated with moving particle (ii) They travel with a speed less than the speed of light (iii) E and B are not associated with these waves Electromagnetic waves (i) They are produced by accelerated charged particles (ii) They travel with the speed of light (iii) E and B are associated with these waves {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E_k}}{/tex} Alternatively, {tex}\lambda \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{E_k}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}=\sqrt{\frac{E_{k 1}}{E_{k 2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{E_{K 1}}{\frac{E_{k 1}}{4}}}=\sqrt{4}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\lambda_2=2 \lambda_1{/tex} So,de Broglie wavelength associated with it become two times.
solution: Matter waves (i) Matter waves are associated with moving particle (ii) They travel with a speed less than the speed of light (iii) E and B are not associated with these waves Electromagnetic waves (i) They are produced by accelerated charged particles (ii) They travel with the speed of light (iii) E and B are associated with these waves {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E_k}}{/tex} Alternatively, {tex}\lambda \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{E_k}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}=\sqrt{\frac{E_{k 1}}{E_{k 2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{E_{K 1}}{\frac{E_{k 1}}{4}}}=\sqrt{4}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\lambda_2=2 \lambda_1{/tex} So,de Broglie wavelength associated with it become two times.
solution_html:
1. Matter waves
    (i) Matter waves are associated with moving particle
    (ii) They travel with a speed less than the speed of light
    (iii) E and B are not associated with these waves
    Electromagnetic waves
    (i) They are produced by accelerated charged particles
    (ii) They travel with the speed of light
    (iii) E and B are associated with these waves
  2. {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E_k}}{/tex}
    Alternatively,
    {tex}\lambda \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{E_k}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}=\sqrt{\frac{E_{k 1}}{E_{k 2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{E_{K 1}}{\frac{E_{k 1}}{4}}}=\sqrt{4}{/tex} = 2
    {tex}\lambda_2=2 \lambda_1{/tex}
    So,de Broglie wavelength associated with it become two times.

explanation: When a charged particle oscillates with some frequency, so, it produces an oscillating electric field in space which further produces an oscillating magnetic field and thus, in turn, is a source of electric field and this process goes on and hence, an electromagnetic wave propagates in the space. Characteristic of electromagnetic waves: No medium is required for the electromagnetic wave to travel through. An electromagnetic wave, although it carries no mass, does carry energy. It also has momentum and can exert pressure (known as radiation pressure). The energy carried by an electromagnetic wave is proportional to the frequency of the wave.
solution: When a charged particle oscillates with some frequency, so, it produces an oscillating electric field in space which further produces an oscillating magnetic field and thus, in turn, is a source of electric field and this process goes on and hence, an electromagnetic wave propagates in the space. solution_html:
1. When a charged particle oscillates with some frequency, so, it produces an oscillating electric field in space which further produces an oscillating magnetic field and thus, in turn, is a source of electric field and this process goes on and hence, an electromagnetic wave propagates in the space.

  2. Characteristic of electromagnetic waves:
    1. No medium is required for the electromagnetic wave to travel through.
    2. An electromagnetic wave, although it carries no mass, does carry energy. It also has momentum and can exert pressure (known as radiation pressure).
    3. The energy carried by an electromagnetic wave is proportional to the frequency of the wave.

  1. Radar
  2. To photograph internal parts of human body
  3. For taking photographs of the sky during night and foggy conditions

question_text: Name the radiation of the electromagnetic spectrum which is used for the following: Radar To photograph internal parts of human body For taking photographs of the sky during night and foggy conditions Give the frequency range in each case.
explanation: Microwaves are used in radar. Because of their small wavelengths, microwaves are not diffracted or bent by objects of normal dimensions. So these waves can be used to beam a signal in a particular direction. X-rays are used to photograph internal parts of a body, X-rays have a high penetrating power. Infrared rays are used for taking photographs of the sky during the night and the foggy conditions. This is because these radiations are readily absorbed by water molecules present in most materials. After absorption, they heat up the materials and their surroundings.

Infrared rays are used for taking photographs of the sky during the night and the foggy conditions. This is because these radiations are readily absorbed by water molecules present in most materials. After absorption, they heat up the materials and their surroundings.

1. Microwaves are used in radar. Because of their small wavelengths, microwaves are not diffracted or bent by objects of normal dimensions. So these waves can be used to beam a signal in a particular direction.
  2. X-rays are used to photograph internal parts of a body, X-rays have a high penetrating power.
  3. Infrared rays are used for taking photographs of the sky during the night and the foggy conditions. This is because these radiations are readily absorbed by water molecules present in most materials. After absorption, they heat up the materials and their surroundings.

question_text: The small ozone layer on top of the stratosphere is crucial for human survival. Why?
explanation: The small ozone layer on the top of the stratosphere absorbs ultraviolet radiations, gamma rays etc., from the sun. Hence these harmful radiations, which can cause genetic damage to the living cells, are prevented from reaching the earth. Thus, the small ozone layer on top of the stratosphere is crucial for human survival.

question_html: A parallel plate capacitor of plate area A each and separation d, is being charged by an ac source. Show that the displacement current inside the capacitor is the same as the current charging the capacitor.
question_text: A parallel plate capacitor of plate area A each and separation d, is being charged by an ac source. Show that the displacement current inside the capacitor is the same as the current charging the capacitor.
explanation: current charging the capacitor is given by ; {tex}i = {dq\over dt}{/tex} displacement current is given between the plates: i d = {tex}\epsilon_0{/tex} {tex}\frac{d \phi_{E}}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\varepsilon_{0} \frac{d(E A)}{d t}{/tex} The electric field between the plates of a capacitor E = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0} A}{/tex} {tex}\therefore i_{d}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{\varepsilon_{0} A} \frac{d q}{d t}{/tex} {tex}i_{d}=\frac{d q}{d t}=i{/tex} Where i is the charging current. This proves that the displacement current inside the capacitor is the same as the current charging the capacitor.
solution: current charging the capacitor is given by ; {tex}i = {dq\over dt}{/tex} displacement current is given between the plates: i d = {tex}\epsilon_0{/tex} {tex}\frac{d \phi_{E}}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\varepsilon_{0} \frac{d(E A)}{d t}{/tex} The electric field between the plates of a capacitor E = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0} A}{/tex} {tex}\therefore i_{d}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{\varepsilon_{0} A} \frac{d q}{d t}{/tex} {tex}i_{d}=\frac{d q}{d t}=i{/tex} Where i is the charging current. This proves that the displacement current inside the capacitor is the same as the current charging the capacitor.
solution_html:
current charging  the capacitor is given by ; {tex}i = {dq\over dt}{/tex} 
displacement current is given between the plates:  id = {tex}\epsilon_0{/tex} {tex}\frac{d \phi_{E}}{d t}{/tex}
= {tex}\varepsilon_{0} \frac{d(E A)}{d t}{/tex}
The electric field between the plates of a capacitor
E = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0} A}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore i_{d}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{\varepsilon_{0} A} \frac{d q}{d t}{/tex}
{tex}i_{d}=\frac{d q}{d t}=i{/tex}
Where i is the charging current. This proves that the displacement current inside the capacitor is the same as the current charging the capacitor.

question_html: Prove that the average energy density of the oscillating electric field is equal to that of the oscillating magnetic field.
question_text: Prove that the average energy density of the oscillating electric field is equal to that of the oscillating magnetic field.
explanation: Average energy density of the electric field {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 E^2 {/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0(c B)^2{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 \frac{1}{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0} B^2{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B^2}{\mu_0}{/tex} = Average energy density of the magnetic field.
solution: Average energy density of the electric field {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 E^2 {/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0(c B)^2{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 \frac{1}{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0} B^2{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B^2}{\mu_0}{/tex} = Average energy density of the magnetic field.
solution_html:
Average energy density of the electric field
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 E^2 {/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0(c B)^2{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 \frac{1}{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0} B^2{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B^2}{\mu_0}{/tex}
= Average energy density of the magnetic field.

subject: Physics
question_html:
When a parallel plate capacitor is being charged by a dc battery, why does a galvanometer show a momentary deflection?
Obtain the expression for the displacement current and show how it is related to conduction current in a steady state.
question_text: When a parallel plate capacitor is being charged by a dc battery, why does a galvanometer show a momentary deflection? Obtain the expression for the displacement current and show how it is related to conduction current in a steady state.
explanation: While being charged, electron flow from battery into the capacitor until the capacitors is completely charged. As a result, galvanometer shows deflection. Between the plates of the capacitor {tex}\emptyset_E=|E| A=\frac{Q}{A \epsilon_O} A=\frac{Q}{\epsilon_O}{/tex} Q changes with time and current {tex}i=\frac{d \emptyset}{d t}{/tex} So, {tex}\frac{d \emptyset_E}{d t}=\frac{1}{\epsilon_O} \frac{d \emptyset}{d t} \Rightarrow i=\epsilon_O \frac{d Q}{d t}{/tex} This is the displacement current i d . Outside the plates, the current in the conductor due to the flow of charges is called conduction current.
solution: While being charged, electron flow from battery into the capacitor until the capacitors is completely charged. As a result, galvanometer shows deflection. Between the plates of the capacitor {tex}\emptyset_E=|E| A=\frac{Q}{A \epsilon_O} A=\frac{Q}{\epsilon_O}{/tex} Q changes with time and current {tex}i=\frac{d \emptyset}{d t}{/tex} So, {tex}\frac{d \emptyset_E}{d t}=\frac{1}{\epsilon_O} \frac{d \emptyset}{d t} \Rightarrow i=\epsilon_O \frac{d Q}{d t}{/tex} This is the displacement current i d . Outside the plates, the current in the conductor due to the flow of charges is called conduction current.
solution_html:
While being charged, electron flow from battery into the capacitor until the capacitors is completely charged.
As a result, galvanometer shows deflection.
Between the plates of the capacitor
{tex}\emptyset_E=|E| A=\frac{Q}{A \epsilon_O} A=\frac{Q}{\epsilon_O}{/tex}
Q changes with time and current {tex}i=\frac{d \emptyset}{d t}{/tex}
So, {tex}\frac{d \emptyset_E}{d t}=\frac{1}{\epsilon_O} \frac{d \emptyset}{d t} \Rightarrow i=\epsilon_O \frac{d Q}{d t}{/tex}
This is the displacement current id.
Outside the plates, the current in the conductor due to the flow of charges is called conduction current.

subject: Physics
question_html:
The magnetic field in a plane electromagnetic wave is given by By = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 sin (1.20 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 z + 3.60 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015t). Calculate the

  1. Energy density associated with the Electromagnetic waves
  2. Speed of the wave.
question_text: The magnetic field in a plane electromagnetic wave is given by B y = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 sin (1.20 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 z + 3.60 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 t). Calculate the Energy density associated with the Electromagnetic waves Speed of the wave.
explanation: Given: Magnetic field in plane electromagnetic wave B y = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 sin (1.20 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 z + 3.60 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 t) Now B 0 = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 T Speed of light c = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m/s From the relation: E 0 = B 0 c = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 E 0 = 36 V/m Now Energy Density can be calculated using: Energy Density of the electromagnetic waves = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex} Energy Density of the electromagnetic waves= {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times\left(8.85 \times 10^{-12}\right) \times(36)^{2}{/tex} Energy Density of electromagnetic waves = 5.74 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 J/m 3 Magnetic field in plane electromagnetic wave B y = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 sin (1.20 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 z + 3.60 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 t) Now k = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 {tex}\omega=3.6 \times 10^{15}{/tex} Since, {tex}\lambda=\frac{2 \pi}{k}{/tex} and {tex}f=\frac{\omega}{2 \pi}{/tex} Hence the speed of wave: v = {tex}\lambda \times f{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\omega}{k}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex}
solution: Given: Magnetic field in plane electromagnetic wave B y = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 sin (1.20 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 z + 3.60 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 t) Now B 0 = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 T Speed of light c = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m/s From the relation: E 0 = B 0 c = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 E 0 = 36 V/m Now Energy Density can be calculated using: Energy Density of the electromagnetic waves = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex} Energy Density of the electromagnetic waves= {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times\left(8.85 \times 10^{-12}\right) \times(36)^{2}{/tex} Energy Density of electromagnetic waves = 5.74 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 J/m 3 Magnetic field in plane electromagnetic wave B y = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 sin (1.20 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 z + 3.60 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 t) Now k = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 {tex}\omega=3.6 \times 10^{15}{/tex} Since, {tex}\lambda=\frac{2 \pi}{k}{/tex} and {tex}f=\frac{\omega}{2 \pi}{/tex} Hence the speed of wave: v = {tex}\lambda \times f{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\omega}{k}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given:

  1. Magnetic field in plane electromagnetic wave By = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 sin (1.20 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 z + 3.60 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015t)
    Now B0 = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8T
    Speed of light c = 3 {tex}\times{/tex}108 m/s
    From the relation: E0 = B0c
    = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108
    E0 = 36 V/m
    Now Energy Density can be calculated using:
    Energy Density of the electromagnetic waves  = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex}
    Energy Density of the electromagnetic waves= {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times\left(8.85 \times 10^{-12}\right) \times(36)^{2}{/tex}
    Energy Density of electromagnetic waves = 5.74 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15 J/m3
  2. Magnetic field in plane electromagnetic wave
    By = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 sin (1.20 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 z + 3.60 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015t)
    Now k = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 107
    {tex}\omega=3.6 \times 10^{15}{/tex}
    Since, {tex}\lambda=\frac{2 \pi}{k}{/tex} and {tex}f=\frac{\omega}{2 \pi}{/tex}
    Hence the speed of wave: v = {tex}\lambda \times f{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{\omega}{k}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Why are infrared waves often called heatwaves? Explain.
  2. What do you understand by the statement, "Electromagnetic waves transport momentum"?
question_text: Why are infrared waves often called heatwaves? Explain. What do you understand by the statement, "Electromagnetic waves transport momentum"?
explanation: Infrared rays are readily absorbed by the (water) molecules in most of the substances and hence increases their thermal motion. Thus increases the internal energy and temperature of the molecules. This is the reason they are often called as heat waves. Electromagnetic waves can set (and sustain) charges in motion. Hence, they are said to transport momentum.
solution: Infrared rays are readily absorbed by the (water) molecules in most of the substances and hence increases their thermal motion. Thus increases the internal energy and temperature of the molecules. This is the reason they are often called as heat waves. Electromagnetic waves can set (and sustain) charges in motion. Hence, they are said to transport momentum.
solution_html:
1. Infrared rays are readily absorbed by the (water) molecules in most of the substances and hence increases their thermal motion. Thus increases the internal energy and temperature of the molecules. This is the reason they are often called as heat waves.
  2. Electromagnetic waves can set (and sustain) charges in motion. Hence, they are said to transport momentum.

subject: Physics
question_html: What do you understand by the statement, Electromagnetic waves transport momentum?
question_text: What do you understand by the statement, Electromagnetic waves transport momentum?
explanation: Electromagnetic waves can set (and sustain) charges in motion. Hence, they are said to transport momentum. (Also accept the following: Electromagnetic waves are known to exert 'radiation pressure'. This pressure is due to the force associated with rate of change of momentum. Hence, EM waves transport momentum).
solution: Electromagnetic waves can set (and sustain) charges in motion. Hence, they are said to transport momentum. (Also accept the following: Electromagnetic waves are known to exert 'radiation pressure'. This pressure is due to the force associated with rate of change of momentum. Hence, EM waves transport momentum).
solution_html:
Electromagnetic waves can set (and sustain) charges in motion. Hence, they are said to transport momentum.
(Also accept the following: Electromagnetic waves are known to exert 'radiation pressure'. This pressure is due to the force associated with rate of change of momentum. Hence, EM waves transport momentum).

subject: Physics
question_html: How does a charge, q oscillating at a certain frequency produce electromagnetic waves? Sketch a schematic diagram depicting electric and magnetic fields for an electromagnetic wave propagating along the Z- direction.
question_text: How does a charge, q oscillating at a certain frequency produce electromagnetic waves? Sketch a schematic diagram depicting electric and magnetic fields for an electromagnetic wave propagating along the Z - direction.
explanation: In general, a moving charge can produce both electric and magnetic fields. So, an oscillating charge can produce oscillating electric and magnetic fields with the same frequency of the oscillating charge. Thus an electromagnetic wave is produced by an oscillating charge directed along the perpendicular to both the electric and magnetic field (two fields are also perpendicular to each other) and the direction of propagation is found from the cross product {tex}\vec E × \vec B{/tex} . In the diagram below, electric and magnetic fields vectors are vibrating along positive X and Y axes respectively with the direction of propagation of the em wave along the positive Z-axis.
solution: In general, a moving charge can produce both electric and magnetic fields. Thus an electromagnetic wave is produced by an oscillating charge directed along the perpendicular to both the electric and magnetic field (two fields are also perpendicular to each other) and the direction of propagation is found from the cross product {tex}\vec E × \vec B{/tex} . In the diagram below, electric and magnetic fields vectors are vibrating along positive X and Y axes respectively with the direction of propagation of the em wave along the positive Z-axis.

Thus an electromagnetic wave is produced by an oscillating charge directed along the perpendicular to both the electric and magnetic field (two fields are also perpendicular to each other) and the direction of propagation is found from the cross product {tex}\vec E × \vec B{/tex}.

subject: Physics
question_html: How is the speed of electromagnetic waves in vacuum determined by the electric and magnetic fields?
question_text: How is the speed of electromagnetic waves in vacuum determined by the electric and magnetic fields?
explanation: The speed of electromagnetic waves in vacuum is determined by the electric and magnetic fields by using the formula: c = {tex}\frac {E_o}{B_o}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {1}{\sqrt {\mu_0 \epsilon_0}}{/tex} where, E 0 and B 0 are maximum electric field and magnetic field components respectively of electromagnetic waves, {tex}\mu_0~ and~ \epsilon_0 {/tex} are permeability and permittivity of vacuum or free space respectively.
solution: The speed of electromagnetic waves in vacuum is determined by the electric and magnetic fields by using the formula: c = {tex}\frac {E_o}{B_o}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {1}{\sqrt {\mu_0 \epsilon_0}}{/tex} where, E 0 and B 0 are maximum electric field and magnetic field components respectively of electromagnetic waves, {tex}\mu_0~ and~ \epsilon_0 {/tex} are permeability and permittivity of vacuum or free space respectively.
solution_html:
The speed of electromagnetic waves in vacuum is determined by the electric and magnetic fields by using the formula:
c = {tex}\frac {E_o}{B_o}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {1}{\sqrt {\mu_0 \epsilon_0}}{/tex}
where, E0 and B0 are maximum electric field and magnetic field components respectively of electromagnetic waves, {tex}\mu_0~ and~ \epsilon_0 {/tex} are permeability and permittivity of vacuum or free space respectively.

question_text: In which directions do the electric and magnetic field vectors oscillate in an electromagnetic wave propagating along the X-axis?
explanation: Electric field vector, {tex}\vec E{/tex} and magnetic field vector, {tex}\vec B{/tex} are always perpendicular to each other and also to direction of propagation of an electromagnetic wave or light. Also, the direction of propagation is parallel to the vector product, {tex}\vec E\times\vec B{/tex} . If wave is propagating along {tex}\hat i{/tex} or X-axis then electirc filed E is vibrating along {tex}\hat j{/tex} or +Y-axis and magnetic field B is vibrating along {tex}\hat k{/tex} or +Z-axis.
solution: Electric field vector, {tex}\vec E{/tex} and magnetic field vector, {tex}\vec B{/tex} are always perpendicular to each other and also to direction of propagation of an electromagnetic wave or light. solution_html:
Electric field vector, {tex}\vec E{/tex} and magnetic field vector, {tex}\vec B{/tex} are always perpendicular to each other and also to direction of propagation of an electromagnetic wave or light. Also, the direction of propagation is parallel to the vector product, {tex}\vec E\times\vec B{/tex} .
 If wave is propagating along {tex}\hat i{/tex} or X-axis then electirc filed E is vibrating along {tex}\hat j{/tex} or +Y-axis and magnetic field B is vibrating along {tex}\hat k{/tex} or +Z-axis.

Therefore, B should be in a direction perpendicular to both x- and y-axes. Using vector algebra, c = E {tex}\times{/tex} B should be along the x-direction. {tex}B=\frac{E}{c}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{6.3 \mathrm{V} / \mathrm{m}}{3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}}=2.1 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{T}{/tex}
To find the direction, we note that E is along y-direction and the wave propagates along x-axis. Therefore, B should be in a direction perpendicular to both x- and y-axes. Using vector algebra, c = E {tex}\times{/tex} B should be along the x-direction.
Since, (+ {tex}\mathbf{\hat j}{/tex}) {tex}\times{/tex} (+ {tex}\hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex}) = {tex}\mathbf{i}{/tex}, B is along the z-direction.E is along y direction and c is along x direction

question_html: Professor C.V Raman surprised his students by suspending freely a tiny light ball in a transparent vacuum chamber by shining a laser beam on it. Which property of EM waves was he exhibiting? Give one more example of this property.
question_text: Professor C.V Raman surprised his students by suspending freely a tiny light ball in a transparent vacuum chamber by shining a laser beam on it. Which property of EM waves was he exhibiting? Give one more example of this property.
explanation: The properties of an electromagnetic wave are the same as other waves. Like other waves, an electromagnetic wave also carries energy and momentum. We know the dual nature of radiation and matter. since it carries momentum, an electromagnetic wave also exerts pressure called radiation pressure. This property of electromagnetic waves helped professor CV Raman surprised his students by suspending freely a tiny light ball in a transparent vacuum chamber by shining a laser beam on it.
solution: The properties of an electromagnetic wave are the same as other waves. Like other waves, an electromagnetic wave also carries energy and momentum. We know the dual nature of radiation and matter. since it carries momentum, an electromagnetic wave also exerts pressure called radiation pressure. This property of electromagnetic waves helped professor CV Raman surprised his students by suspending freely a tiny light ball in a transparent vacuum chamber by shining a laser beam on it.
solution_html:
The properties of an electromagnetic wave are the same as other waves. Like other waves, an electromagnetic wave also carries energy and momentum. We know the dual nature of radiation and matter.
since it carries momentum, an electromagnetic wave also exerts pressure called radiation pressure. This property of electromagnetic waves helped professor CV Raman surprised his students by suspending freely a tiny light ball in a transparent vacuum chamber by shining a laser beam on it.

question_html: Show, by giving a simple example, how EM waves carry energy and momentum.
question_text: Show, by giving a simple example, how EM waves carry energy and momentum.
explanation: Electromagnetic radiation is the transfer of energy by electromagnetic waves. Basically it is the movement of waves from one place to another. Consider a plane perpendicular to the direction of propagation of the wave. An electric charge, on the plane, will be set in motion by the electric and magnetic fields of EM wave, incident on this plane. This illustrates that EM waves carry energy and momentum.

Uses: (any one) Gamma-radiations are used in medical treatment and for checking flaws in metal castings and for detection by photographic plates or radiation detectors. X-rays are used in medicine and dentistry and may be detected using photographic film. The wavelength range of 10 4 m < {tex}\lambda{/tex} < 10 -6 m shows the presence of Infrared, Visible and Microwave. Uses : (any one) Infrared radiation is useful for haze photography and is used by Earth resource satellites to detect healthy crops. It can be used to study human and animal body heat patterns. Visible light affects a photographic film, stimulates the retina in the eye and causes photosynthesis in plants. Microwaves are used in radar, telemetry and electron spin resonance studies and in microwave ovens.

question_html: How does Ampere-Maxwell law explain the flow of current through a capacitor when it is being charged by a battery? Write the expression for the displacement current in terms of the rate of change of electric flux.
question_text: How does Ampere-Maxwell law explain the flow of current through a capacitor when it is being charged by a battery? Write the expression for the displacement current in terms of the rate of change of electric flux.
explanation: When we charge a capacitor with the help of a cell, current flows all through the circuit except the part of a circuit which lies between two plates of the capacitor. There is no current found in that gap. But it was found that an electric field exists inside the plates, therefore, indicating the existence of a magnetic field which leads to inconsistency in the Ampere’s circuital law. When a battery is attached to a capacitor, conduction current flow in wire outside the capacitor, then through the capacitor the Electric flux ϕ E = EA. when {tex}E={Q\over \epsilon _0 A}{/tex} and {tex}Q= \epsilon_0 E A {/tex} so {tex}I={dQ\over dt}{/tex} = {tex}\epsilon _0 d\phi\over dt{/tex} This maintains the current in the capacitor. During charging, the electric flux between the plates of the capacitor keeps on changing, this results in the production of a displacement current between the plates. {tex}I_{d}=\varepsilon_{0} \frac{d \phi_{E}}{d t}\left(I_{d}=\varepsilon_{0} A \frac{d E}{d t}\right){/tex}
solution: When we charge a capacitor with the help of a cell, current flows all through the circuit except the part of a circuit which lies between two plates of the capacitor. There is no current found in that gap. But it was found that an electric field exists inside the plates, therefore, indicating the existence of a magnetic field which leads to inconsistency in the Ampere’s circuital law. When a battery is attached to a capacitor, conduction current flow in wire outside the capacitor, then through the capacitor the Electric flux ϕ E = EA. when {tex}E={Q\over \epsilon _0 A}{/tex} and {tex}Q= \epsilon_0 E A {/tex} so {tex}I={dQ\over dt}{/tex} = {tex}\epsilon _0 d\phi\over dt{/tex} This maintains the current in the capacitor. During charging, the electric flux between the plates of the capacitor keeps on changing, this results in the production of a displacement current between the plates. {tex}I_{d}=\varepsilon_{0} \frac{d \phi_{E}}{d t}\left(I_{d}=\varepsilon_{0} A \frac{d E}{d t}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
When we charge a capacitor with the help of a cell, current flows all through the circuit except the part of a circuit which lies between two plates of the capacitor. There is no current found in that gap. But it was found that an electric field exists inside the plates, therefore, indicating the existence of a magnetic field which leads to inconsistency in the Ampere’s circuital law.
When a battery is attached to a capacitor, conduction current flow in wire outside the capacitor, then through the capacitor the Electric flux ϕE = EA.
when {tex}E={Q\over \epsilon _0 A}{/tex} and {tex}Q= \epsilon_0 E A {/tex} 
so {tex}I={dQ\over dt}{/tex}={tex}\epsilon _0 d\phi\over dt{/tex}
This maintains the current in the capacitor.
During charging, the electric flux between the plates of the capacitor keeps on changing, this results in the production of a displacement current between the plates.
{tex}I_{d}=\varepsilon_{0} \frac{d \phi_{E}}{d t}\left(I_{d}=\varepsilon_{0} A \frac{d E}{d t}\right){/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Why does current in a steady state not flow in a capacitor connected across a battery? However momentary current does flow during the charging or discharging of the capacitor. Explain.
question_text: Why does current in a steady state not flow in a capacitor connected across a battery? However momentary current does flow during the charging or discharging of the capacitor. Explain.
explanation: In steady state conditions, the voltage across the capacitor nearly equals to the voltage of the charging source. Hence net voltage acting in the circuit is zero. Due to this there is no flow of charge (current) through the circuit and hence the capacitor. Now we know for an uncharged capacitor the potential difference across the capacitor is zero. So when it is connected to a battery, the battery begins to charge it by withdrawing the free electrons from one plate connected with the positive terminal of the battery of the capacitance and deposit on the other plate of the capacitor. So, due to this flow of charge, there exist a momentary current while charging of the capacitor. Now when the capacitor is fully charged i.e. voltage across the capacitor nearly equals to the voltage of the charging source, the current in the circuit vanishes. Now in case of discharging of a charged capacitor, the battery gets replaced by a wire of say resistance R. Hence the capacitor itself acts as a battery now and flow of charge exists in the circuit from negative plate to the positive plate of the battery. This leads to momentary current in the circuit. As soon as the all the positive charges on one plate gets neutralised by the negative charges on the other plate, the flow of charge stops and hence the current.
solution: In steady state conditions, the voltage across the capacitor nearly equals to the voltage of the charging source. Hence net voltage acting in the circuit is zero. Due to this there is no flow of charge (current) through the circuit and hence the capacitor. Now we know for an uncharged capacitor the potential difference across the capacitor is zero. So when it is connected to a battery, the battery begins to charge it by withdrawing the free electrons from one plate connected with the positive terminal of the battery of the capacitance and deposit on the other plate of the capacitor. So, due to this flow of charge, there exist a momentary current while charging of the capacitor. Now when the capacitor is fully charged i.e. voltage across the capacitor nearly equals to the voltage of the charging source, the current in the circuit vanishes. Now in case of discharging of a charged capacitor, the battery gets replaced by a wire of say resistance R. Hence the capacitor itself acts as a battery now and flow of charge exists in the circuit from negative plate to the positive plate of the battery. This leads to momentary current in the circuit. As soon as the all the positive charges on one plate gets neutralised by the negative charges on the other plate, the flow of charge stops and hence the current.
solution_html:
In steady state conditions, the voltage across the capacitor nearly equals to the voltage of the charging source. Hence net voltage acting in the circuit is zero. Due to this there is no flow of charge (current) through the circuit and hence the capacitor.
Now we know for an uncharged capacitor the potential difference across the capacitor is zero. So when it is connected to a battery, the battery begins to charge it by withdrawing the free electrons from one plate connected with the positive terminal of the battery of the capacitance and deposit on the other plate of the capacitor. So, due to this flow of charge, there exist a momentary current while charging of the capacitor. Now when the capacitor is fully charged i.e. voltage across the capacitor nearly equals to the voltage of the charging source, the current in the circuit vanishes.
Now in case of discharging of a charged capacitor, the battery gets replaced by a wire of say resistance R. Hence the capacitor itself acts as a battery now and flow of charge exists in the circuit from negative plate to the positive plate of the battery. This leads to momentary current in the circuit. As soon as the all the positive charges on one plate gets neutralised by the negative charges on the other plate, the flow of charge stops and hence the current.
