subject: Physics
question_html: Give a brief history of the observation of electromagnetic waves.
question_text: Give a brief history of the observation of electromagnetic waves.
explanation: History of electromagnetic waves : In 1865, Maxwell suggested that a changing electric field gives rise to a changing magnetic field and vice - versa. It means that when either of the fields changes with time, the other field is induced in space. The superposition of these time varying electric and magnetic field give rise to electromagnetic disturbance. Such disturbance can propagate through the space even in the absence of material medium. In other words, electromagnetic disturbance (or wave ) can propagate through vacuum. In 1887, a German Scientist Hertz, first of all gave a practical demonstration of the production of electromagnetic waves using an oscillatory circuit. However, the electromagnetic waves produced by Hertz were to large wavelength and hence small frequency which could not be detected over a large distance. Just seven years after Hertz.s observation, an Indian Scientist ranging from 5 mm to 25 mm. But his experiment remained confined to the laboratory. In 1895, Marconi made an important discovery which made electromagnetic waves of commericial value. He was successful in transmitting electromagnetic waves upto a few kilometers.

question_text: What feature of electromagnetic waves led Maxwell to conclude that light itself is an electromagnetic wave?
explanation: The emergence of the speed of light from purely electromagnetic consideration led Maxwell to conclude that light waves are electromagnetic in nature.

subject: Physics
question_html: How does radar help in determining the distance of a distant object?
question_text: How does radar help in determining the distance of a distant object?
explanation: The high-frequency microwaves are sent towards the distant object and the reflected waves are picked up by a receiver. If the waves come back in time t, then the distance of the distant object is {tex}D=\frac{c t}{2}{/tex} , where c is the speed of light.
solution: The high-frequency microwaves are sent towards the distant object and the reflected waves are picked up by a receiver. If the waves come back in time t, then the distance of the distant object is {tex}D=\frac{c t}{2}{/tex} , where c is the speed of light.
solution_html: The high-frequency microwaves are sent towards the distant object and the reflected waves are picked up by a receiver. If the waves come back in time t, then the distance of the distant object is {tex}D=\frac{c t}{2}{/tex}, where c is the speed of light.

subject: Physics
question_html: What modification was made by Maxwell in Ampere's circuital law?
question_text: What modification was made by Maxwell in Ampere's circuital law?
explanation: A displacement current, {tex}I_{D}=\varepsilon_{0} \frac{d \phi_{E}}{d t}{/tex} was added to the current term of Ampere circuital law to make it logically consistent so that the modified form of Ampere's circuital law is {tex}\int \vec{B} \cdot {d}\vec{ l}=\mu_{0}\left(I_{C}+I_{D}\right){/tex} .
solution: A displacement current, {tex}I_{D}=\varepsilon_{0} \frac{d \phi_{E}}{d t}{/tex} was added to the current term of Ampere circuital law to make it logically consistent so that the modified form of Ampere's circuital law is {tex}\int \vec{B} \cdot {d}\vec{ l}=\mu_{0}\left(I_{C}+I_{D}\right){/tex} .
solution_html: A displacement current, {tex}I_{D}=\varepsilon_{0} \frac{d \phi_{E}}{d t}{/tex} was added to the current term of Ampere circuital law to make it logically consistent so that the modified form of Ampere's circuital law is {tex}\int \vec{B} \cdot {d}\vec{ l}=\mu_{0}\left(I_{C}+I_{D}\right){/tex}.

subject: Physics
question_html: What is the greenhouse effect?
question_text: What is the greenhouse effect ?
explanation: The greenhouse effect is the phenomenon that keeps the earth's surface warm at night. The earth reflects back the infrared part of solar radiation. Infrared rays are reflected back by low lying clouds and lower atmosphere and keep the earth's surface warm at night.
solution: The greenhouse effect is the phenomenon that keeps the earth's surface warm at night. The earth reflects back the infrared part of solar radiation. Infrared rays are reflected back by low lying clouds and lower atmosphere and keep the earth's surface warm at night.
solution_html: The greenhouse effect is the phenomenon that keeps the earth's surface warm at night. The earth reflects back the infrared part of solar radiation. Infrared rays are reflected back by low lying clouds and lower atmosphere and keep the earth's surface warm at night.

question_html: A parallel plate capacitor is charged by a current of 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 A displaced between the plates of the capacitor. When the discharge of the capacitor takes place through a resistance, the rate of change of electric flux (in Wb/s) will be:
question_text: A parallel plate capacitor is charged by a current of 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 A displaced between the plates of the capacitor. When the discharge of the capacitor takes place through a resistance, the rate of change of electric flux (in Wb/s) will be:

options.explanation: Displacement current, {tex}I_{d}=\varepsilon_{0} \frac{d \phi_{E}}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d \phi}{d t}=\frac{I_{d}}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{2 \times 10^{-7}}{8.85 \times 10^{-12}}{/tex} = 2.26 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 Wb/s

explanation: Displacement current, {tex}I_{d}=\varepsilon_{0} \frac{d \phi_{E}}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d \phi}{d t}=\frac{I_{d}}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{2 \times 10^{-7}}{8.85 \times 10^{-12}}{/tex} = 2.26 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 Wb/s

subject: Physics
question_html: What oscillates in electromagnetic waves? Are these waves transverse or longitudinal?
question_text: What oscillates in electromagnetic waves? Are these waves transverse or longitudinal?
explanation: In e.m. waves, electric and magnetic fields oscillate in mutually perpendicular directions. These waves are transverse in nature.
solution: In e.m. waves, electric and magnetic fields oscillate in mutually perpendicular directions. These waves are transverse in nature.
solution_html:
1. In e.m. waves, electric and magnetic fields oscillate in mutually perpendicular directions.
  2. These waves are transverse in nature.
